Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic most of the time. I work part-time to help cover my expenses, so I'd squeeze in studying whenever I could—usually late at night or between shifts. I'm someone who learns best by doing things hands-on, so I'd often grab materials and practice problems rather than just reading. Some weeks I'd manage maybe 15-20 hours of studying, other weeks it was way less depending on what else was going on in my life.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on what was due and what shifts I had at work. I couldn't afford to have a set schedule because my work hours changed week to week. When deadlines were close, I'd basically cram everything into a few intense sessions. It wasn't ideal, but it's what I had to work with given my situation.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I knew something was due or when I needed to check what assignments I was missing. Sometimes my peer study group would message me asking about something specific, and I'd hop on to look it up. I wasn't someone who logged in just to browse—I went in with a purpose because my time was limited.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. It never became a natural habit for me because I was always juggling so many things. The closest it got to routine was when my study group started meeting regularly and we'd all check the discussion boards together. But even then, I'd often just ask one of them what was posted rather than logging in myself.
Q5: The discussion posts were actually pretty helpful because I could learn from what other students were thinking and struggling with. When I did watch the videos, I found them useful for understanding concepts I couldn't grasp from reading alone. I'm a hands-on learner, so anything that showed me how to actually do something rather than just explaining theory worked better for me.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the optional readings and supplementary materials. With limited time and low resources, I had to prioritize what would actually help me pass. Some of the longer video lectures I'd skip through or just not watch at all because I couldn't sit through hours of content when I had work in a few hours.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the assignments or exams. If I could tell it would help me actually complete something or understand a concept I was stuck on, I'd engage with it. Anything that felt like busy work or wasn't clearly connected to my grade got pushed aside because I just didn't have the luxury of extra time.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt like a nice-to-have that I could never actually get to. Required ones had deadlines and consequences, so they got priority. I always told myself I'd go back to the optional stuff when I had time, but that time never really came. It's not that I didn't want to learn more—I just had to be strategic about where I put my energy.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a ton of PDFs and materials to study offline because I don't always have reliable internet access at home. I'd print stuff out at the library when I could afford it, or study from notes I made during the videos. A lot of my actual learning happened with physical materials spread out on my bed or at the break room at work.
Q10: There were many times I was studying hard but it wouldn't show online at all. I'd have downloaded lecture slides and be reviewing them on the bus to work, or I'd be working through practice problems I'd written out by hand. My peer group would meet in person sometimes and we'd work through concepts together—none of that shows up in any system.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once to get the gist, then download any related materials and never go back to the video itself. I'd work from my notes and the PDFs for days or weeks after. The LMS would show I accessed something once, but I was actually using that material repeatedly offline.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when work picked up extra shifts and I was exhausted. I also had some family stuff going on that pulled my attention away. During those times, I'd miss classes and fall behind on assignments. It wasn't that I didn't care—I was just stretched too thin and something had to give.
Q13: Definitely. When I was tired from work, I'd log in and just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities were the biggest factor—I had to choose between studying and earning money to eat, and sometimes survival won. The confusion from missing classes also made it harder to engage because I felt lost on what I was supposed to be doing.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd open up materials and my brain just wasn't there. I'd scroll through readings or let a video play while I was half-asleep or thinking about other problems. Technically I was 'engaging' with the content, but I wasn't actually absorbing anything meaningful. It was more like going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I'd like to admit, even knowing they were important. Sometimes it was because I didn't understand the material well enough and felt overwhelmed starting. Other times I was just so tired that I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow would turn into next week. The stress of being behind made it even harder to start.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled everything. I'd have bursts of activity right before something was due, cramming to get it done. Without a deadline, I probably wouldn't have engaged with half the material I did. It's not the healthiest approach, but when you're managing a lot, deadlines are what force you to prioritize.
Q17: When I got feedback that was encouraging or showed me where I went wrong, it motivated me to try harder on the next thing. My peer support group was really helpful with this—they'd give me informal feedback and help me understand what I was missing. Official feedback from instructors was sometimes slow, so I couldn't always use it to improve before the next deadline.
Q18: The structure was honestly a bit overwhelming with 16 courses worth of content. I struggled to keep track of what was due when, and the pacing didn't always match my availability. When things were clearly laid out and I knew exactly what was expected, I did better. But when it was vague or there were too many moving parts, I'd fall behind.
Q19: Having videos I could download and watch offline made things easier for me. What made it harder was when activities required real-time participation or consistent internet access, which I don't always have. Also, when assignments had really specific formatting requirements or needed software I didn't have access to, that created extra barriers.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's trying hard but struggling to actually complete things. You might be logging in, watching videos, reading materials, genuinely trying to learn—but then life gets in the way of finishing the assignments. High activity doesn't always mean you have the time or resources to produce the final work.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like my experience. My exam score was decent and I passed, but my LMS activity was pretty low. I did a lot of learning offline and through my peer group. The system can't see me studying from printed notes at 2 AM or quizzing each other before work. Low activity doesn't mean low effort or low learning.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, which gives a surface-level picture of engagement. But they miss the quality of that engagement—whether you were actually focused or just going through motions. They also completely miss offline studying, peer collaboration, and the real-life barriers that affect how someone shows up in the data.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and putting in effort to learn, even when it's hard. For me, it's not just about logging in or completing tasks—it's about genuinely trying to grow and improve. Sometimes I was deeply engaged with the content even when my attendance and completion rates didn't show it.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I fought to stay in this course despite everything working against me. It wouldn't show the late nights studying after exhausting shifts, or how my peer group kept me going when I wanted to give up. It wouldn't capture that I'm here because I'm trying to build a better future, even if my numbers don't look impressive on paper.
